The AI/ML talent market is one of the most exciting and rewarding sectors to work within, whilst also being one of the most challenging spaces in recruitment right now.

Every company wants to accelerate its AI strategy, whether that’s building proprietary LLMs, scaling machine learning infrastructure, hiring MLOps specialists, or bringing in data scientists who can turn experimentation into commercial impact.

The problem? Demand is massively outpacing supply.

From a talent acquisition perspective, companies are facing several major challenges:

A limited pool of experienced AI/ML professionals, particularly those with hands-on expertise in areas like Generative AI, Computer Vision, NLP, and MLOps.

Intense competition from Big Tech, VC-backed startups, and global consultancies are driving salaries higher and shrinking candidate availability.
Lengthy internal hiring processes are causing businesses to lose top talent to faster-moving competitors.

A growing skills gap where many organisations know they need AI talent, but aren’t always clear on what “good” looks like in such a rapidly evolving market.

This is where specialist recruitment agencies can create real value.
Agencies that truly understand the AI/ML landscape can help businesses solve these challenges by:

Providing access to passive talent networks that companies can’t reach through traditional job ads.

Advising on realistic salary benchmarking and market conditions.

Helping define hiring roadmaps based on business goals, whether that’s hiring one founding ML engineer or building an entire AI function.

Reducing time-to-hire by delivering pre-qualified candidates faster.

Offering flexible hiring solutions across permanent, contract, and project-based talent needs.

The companies that win in AI won’t necessarily be the ones with the biggest budgets; they’ll be the ones that move quickly, hire strategically, and partner with experts who understand the market.

The AI race is moving fast. Hiring strategies need to move even faster.

What challenges are you seeing in the AI/ML hiring market right now?

Most CS teams fall into one of two camps.

They either pay $50K+ a year for a platform like Gainsight and wait six weeks for their first health score — or they rely on gut feel, a two-year-old spreadsheet, and whoever shouts loudest in the Friday standup.

There's almost nothing in between. And the gap is quietly costing companies their renewals.

The data you need to score account health already exists. It's sitting in your product database, your helpdesk, your CRM, your billing system. The problem isn't access. It's stitching it together every Monday without hiring a CS Ops lead or burning three months on a platform rollout.

A few things worth knowing if you're building your first one:
— Usage is almost always the strongest predictor of churn. Weight it around 40%.
— Start with three signals you can collect cleanly, not eight you half-collect.
— Your noisiest customers are often your healthiest ones. Support volume alone will mislead you.
— A score that lives in a dashboard nobody opens is a vanity metric. Tie it to a CSM action, or don't bother.

The good news: AI can do the stitching now. What used to need a CS Ops hire and a six-figure contract can run on CSV exports from tools you already own, refreshed every Monday before standup. Faster. More thorough. A fraction of the cost.
We wrote up the practical version — the four signals that actually predict churn, the weights, what a working model looks like: https://lnkd.in/eXdvqjiJ

If you had to build a health score tomorrow using only three signals, which three would you pick?

#CustomerSuccess #Data #DataAnalytics #AIDataAnalyst

The DMV 2026 Construction Report: 3 Shifts Every Leader Must Navigate

The skyline of DC, Maryland, and Northern Virginia is undergoing a fundamental transformation. We aren't just building faster; we are building smarter.

For owners, developers, and GCs, the "old way" of staffing is a liability. To build an Empire in 2026, you must understand these three educational shifts in our regional market:

1. The "Gigawatt" Technical Leap ⚡
The DMV, specifically Northern Virginia is no longer just "Data Center Alley." It is the global epicenter of AI-ready infrastructure.

- The Reality: Standard data centers cost $10M per MW; AI-optimized facilities are now crossing $20M per MW.

- The Talent Need: We are seeing a massive demand for MEP Directors and Commissioning Agents who understand liquid cooling and 100kW rack densities.

2. Infrastructure as a "Predictive" System 🏗️

From the $5.9B Frederick Douglass Tunnel to the Long Bridge Expansion, projects are moving from static blueprints to "Living Models."

- The Shift: 2026 is the year Digital Twins and BIM 6.0 became the contract standard.

- The Lesson: Success in 2026 is measured by Digital Project Delivery (DPD). Firms that don't hire leaders fluent in AI-driven scheduling and reality capture are already 20% over budget before they break ground.

3. The Retirement "Cliff" vs. The Skill Gap 📉
The U.S. construction industry needs 349,000 net new workers in 2026 just to maintain equilibrium.

- The Crisis: Nearly 20% of skilled trades (especially electricians) are over age 55 and nearing retirement.

- The Strategy: You cannot "job board" your way out of a retirement cliff. You need to find passive, elite talent, the 1% of leaders who can mentor the next generation while managing high-voltage utility interconnections.

My Take for HDi Partners:
Success in this market isn't about finding "a person"; it's about securing a Competitive Advantage. At HeatherDaniel International, we provide the leadership that ensures your project doesn't become another "schedule overrun" statistic.

Building for the next generation requires investing in the right people today.

Are you hiring for 2024 problems, or 2030 solutions? #DMVConstruction #DataCenters #Infrastructure #BuildingTheEmpire #HDi #MarketIntelligence #ExecutiveSearch #AEC

Role: Senior IT AI/ML Engineer - Data Analytics
Send me your Resume to my E- sukesh@nebulapartners.com 

Job Summary

As a Senior IT AI/ML Engineer, you will be responsible for the hands-on development, and implementation of AI-powered solutions that solve challenging business problems across IT and various business functions (Sales, Product, Finance & Marketing). You will work closely with Principal and Data leads to translate high-level strategies into concrete AI solution designs and robust, scalable, and ethical implementations. This role demands deep technical expertise, a strong ability to solve problems, and collaborate with other engineers, leveraging our AI Platform to deliver measurable business impact.

AI Solution Implementation: Design and implementation of AI solutions, translating business problems into AI designs, and managing model selection, data requirements, and integration for AI applications.
Platform Development: Develop and implement core components of the enterprise AI/ML platform, ensuring scalability and security. Contribute to the lifecycle of traditional and Generative AI model deployment and real-time inference systems.

Most blockchain platforms fail for one reason: they optimize for the tech, not the human.

We worked with a fractional real estate platform. They had everything.

Smart contracts? ✓
Wallet integration? ✓
On-chain recording? ✓

But investors were confused.

They didn't care about gas fees or contract addresses. They just wanted to know: "Should I buy more of this property?"
 
So we flipped it.

Built an AI agent that actually talks to investors. Understands their portfolio. Answers real questions. Natural language.

Transactions still hit the blockchain. Audits still bulletproof. But the investor? Never thinks about blockchain at all.

Result: 24/7 investment decisions without calling support. Company scaled without hiring.

Here's the kicker: building the blockchain was easy. Making it invisible? That was the hard part.

So if you're building fintech or any platform with complex decisions, ask yourself: Are you optimizing for the tech, or the person using it?

Most PE is hiring the wrong AI role.

The default move right now is a full-time Chief AI Officer. $280K to $380K base, plus a 15 to 30% bonus, plus RSUs. Slot them into every portfolio company, demand quarterly AI progress, check the LP-deck box.

The market signals pushing firms toward that move are loud. Harvey.AI, the legal AI platform built for law firms (contract drafting, research, case analysis, the full knowledge-work stack), just raised $200M at an $11B valuation. They cover 50% of the Am Law 100. Legal AI went from "interesting" to "institutional" in under 18 months. CAIO penetration globally is up from 11% to 26% of organizations in the last two years. CAIO postings are up 400% since 2023.

Every mid-market PE firm I talk to is feeling the pressure. The LP deck needs an AI story. The portfolio needs an AI leader. The playbook from the last cycle of operations talent looks like the move: hire a full-time exec, put them in every portco, demand quarterly progress.

ZRG Partners, one of the most active executive search firms in this space, published a counter-thesis earlier this quarter: stop hiring a CAIO first. Their argument is that sub-$500M portfolio companies don't have the AI ops maturity to support a full-time hire. A fractional or consultancy model de-risks the role until the playbook is proven.

I've been pitching this exact thesis for 12 months. The search firms are catching up.

The sequence that's working for the portfolios I advise:
1. Profit Velocity Blueprint audit per portco to map where AI payoff actually sits
2. Fractional CAIO engagement across 2-3 portcos to ship the highest-ROI opportunities
3. Benchmark the results
4. THEN make the full-time hiring call, informed by 6-9 months of real data

The firms that skip to step 4 are paying six-figure salaries for a role that hasn't been scoped yet. The firms that start with 1-2 are spending a tenth of that and learning where the actual work is.

If you run a mid-market PE fund or operate at the portco level and you're building the AI playbook right now, the fractional-first sequence compounds. The rush to hire doesn't.

Save this post if you're getting pressure to "do AI" at the portfolio level this quarter.

Big news for the contingent workforce world stemming out of Anthropic - a contract worker just stole access to their most powerful AI model.

Bloomberg reported this week that unauthorized users gained access to Claude Mythos, the AI model Anthropic deemed too dangerous to release publicly because it can autonomously find and exploit vulnerabilities in every major operating system and browser.

How did they get in? Through a third-party contractor.

The person had legitimate access to Anthropic's systems as a contract worker performing AI model evaluations. They used that access, combined with information leaked in a recent data breach from an AI training startup, to locate and access Mythos - along with several other unreleased Anthropic models.

This happened on the same day Anthropic announced Project Glasswing, their carefully controlled program to give only AWS, Apple, Microsoft, and a handful of other vetted organizations access to the model.

The group claims they're not using it for cyberattacks - just "playing around" and building simple websites to avoid detection. But that's not really the point.
The point is that insider threats through contingent workers are becoming one of the most common attack vectors large enterprises face. And most firms have no systematic way to prevent it.

You can have world-class perimeter security, mandatory background checks, and strict access controls. But if a fraudulent contractor makes it through your hiring process, none of that matters. They're already inside.

This is why contingent workforce programs at major enterprises are starting to treat candidate verification with the same rigor they apply to cybersecurity. Because the threat is structurally identical - and the damage can be just as severe.

California's privacy agency just opened a comment window some privacy pros may miss, and it is a rulemaking that belongs on your calendar this month.

On April 20, the CPPA issued an Invitation for Preliminary Comments on employee data under the CCPA. The CPPAis asking how the regulation should be scoped before it writes any text, which is the stage where comments actually move language. The window closes at 5 p.m. PT on May 20, and comments should be sent to regulations@cppa.ca.gov.

As everyone remembers, the CCPA carve-out for employees and applicants sunset in January 2023. Since 2023, employee data is treated like general consumer data: notice at collection for applicants, processor contracts for your HRIS, automated decision-making in hiring and termination, etc. But now the CPPA is considering whether employee data should be treated differently. 

If you need a reason to care, look at Mercor. The $10 billion AI hiring platform that provides the frontier AI models with training data. Last month Mercor experienced a data breach through a compromised API, which allegedly exposed 40,000 applicants' Social Security numbers, biometric data, and recorded video interviews. Multiple class actions have been filed, customers have paused work, and others are "investigating their exposure," which is the phrase companies tend to use when they are still deciding whether they want to be next.

Most privacy programs that do not operate in the EU have not mapped their employee data like they have done on the consumer side, meaning data mapping, vendor controls, the impact assessments, and breach-notification playbook. While some may take a wait-and-see approach, employee data is increasingly a point of focus from regulators. Therefore, whether employee data will see increased protections is less of an "if" and more of a "when."

If you are drafting a preliminary comment, I would be glad to discuss! 
#PrivacyLaw #CCPA #EmploymentPrivacy #AIGovernance

There’s a misrepresentation problem brewing in the wedding industry, and we all need to be paying attention.

This week, I came across a story about a photographer who posted a stunning wedding on social media. Perfectly curated. Beautifully styled. The kind of imagery that makes you stop scrolling. The only problem? The wedding never happened. The couple didn’t exist. Every image was AI-generated and presented as real, documented work.

And this isn’t isolated. I’ve seen the same pattern popping up across the creative industry:   designers showcasing “designs” that areAI generated and they can’t actually execute. Florists posting installations that were never built. People uploading other creatives’ work into AI tools and spinning out derivative “portfolios” from them.

Hmm

Let me say this now: presenting AI-generated work as real, completed work is misrepresentation. Depending on the facts, it can also cross into false advertising, deceptive trade practices, copyright infringement (when you’re feeding someone else’s images in as the source material), and in the worst cases, fraud. People (for instance Couples) are making five and six-figure hiring decisions based on portfolios. If that portfolio is fiction, that is a legal problem.

We are operating in a loosely regulated space right now. Courts are handing down conflicting rulings on AI-generated content, training data, copyright, and disclosure. What’s permissible today may not be tomorrow. More legislation is coming. More lawsuits are coming. The people who will be protected are the ones who built their businesses on transparency from the jump.

Do not overpromise. Do not sell an aesthetic AI created that you cannot actually execute.

Do not pass AI imagery off as documented work. If you’re using AI for mood boards or concept visuals, label it. Clearly.

Do not feed other creatives’ work into AI tools to generate your “own” content. That’s not inspiration. That’s infringement.

Update your contracts. Your client agreements should address AI use - what you will and won’t use it for, disclosure obligations, and IP ownership of anything AI-assisted.

Industries such as the event and hospitality industry run on trust. Clients trust you with their most precious moments. Let’s not let a shortcut blow that up.

Anyway, I’m watching this space closely and so should you.

I built the BizLoon Inbox Doctor because I watched small business owners get quoted $3,000-5,000 to fix their email deliverability.

Email authentication involves multiple layers of DNS records, alignment rules, and provider-specific requirements that change every year. Most small businesses do not have a deliverability person on staff, and hiring one costs more than the problem feels like it should.

That gap is what the Inbox Doctor closes. It scans your domain, identifies exactly what is broken, and walks you through fixing it with AI-guided steps tailored to your specific DNS provider and email setup. What used to require an expensive consultant and weeks of back-and-forth now happens in one focused session.

So we built a scanner that tells you exactly what is broken in 30 seconds. And if you want help fixing it, we charge $98 for a guided fix or $498 for done-for-you. Not $5,000.

That is the whole pitch. No contracts. No monthly minimums on the fix side. Just see what is broken, then decide if you want help.

We are still early. Building in public. If you know a small business owner whose emails are landing in spam, send them to bizloon.com. It is free and it might save them a client.

How Rho 10xed Growth while everybody was going backward with AI SDRs.

In a world of endless wait times, Bureacracy, slow replies and flagrant spam…. 

While companies obsessed with automating bad processes and hiring AI SDRs, Rho’s founders did the complete opposite.

- Response time: under 1 minute (live chat, real person)
- First call → signed contract: same day
- Account opening: minutes
- Onboarding: same day 

The Relationship
Your first contact owns your entire journey. Every follow-up routes directly to them. No handoffs. One person, full ownership. Every time you call to upgrade you get patched through to your OG contact who sells you and puts you straight through to same day fulfilment. 

The Result
$100M ARR + $350M in funding 

The lesson I think here is that relationships and partnerships are what actually scales. RHO went hard on putting on events, hiring bubbly sales people with large social groups, then they scaled through networks.

What I like about this is that obviously the had elite human capital building AI to run the business but I love that up front was all personal relationship selling. 

They used AI to build world class systems that sped up speed to lead, call routing and onboarding.

They didnt replace humans they just enhanced their systems to work better than any human possibly could but they key was each customer felt like they had a personal contact within the company. 

Being able to take a warm lead and get them sold and onboarded in a day in the heavily regulated Fintech industry is a huge achievement and gives us all something to think about.

But even more to learn from I think is that rather then just feeling like a number on hold for 35 minutes, customers could get straight through and talk to their person within 1 minute. 

Could this be a sign of a return to human first service in the age of AI? 

If you look at the legendary Bird Dog company  Noah Jacobs 🐦🐕  is building. They are finding a similar story.

Relationships are what ultimately sell good tech.

Most SaaS founders think EU AI Act risk depends on where their servers sit.

It doesn’t.

If your EU customer uses your AI product in hiring, credit, health, education, policing, or other high-risk workflows, Article 26 can pull you into serious deployer obligations.

Not because you built the model.

Because your product is being used inside the EU.

I made a simple 10-minute SaaS Compliance Quadrant to help founders classify where they stand:

Q1: Full-Stack Provider  
Q2: Integrator  
Q3: Builder  
Q4: Wrapper  

The dangerous trap?

Many SaaS teams are actually Q2 — Integrator — but they behave like Q4 — Wrapper.

That mistake can create evidence gaps, contract gaps, oversight gaps, and reporting gaps.

Less than 100 days.

Start with classification.

Comment “QUADRANT” and I’ll share the one-page diagnostic template.

89% of staffing firm's blog content is aimed at people who can't hire.

I analyzed 646 mid-market staffing firms. Here's what they actually publish:
47% — candidate career advice 
18% — company news, awards, office openings 
24% — generic industry commentary 
11% — written for the person who actually hires a staffing agency

Candidates don't sign contracts. Hiring managers do. VPs of HR do. Procurement does.

The average staffing blog is 4x more focused on people who can't buy than people who can.

The candidate-and-news pattern is understandable. It's easy to produce. An award comes in, you write a post. An office opens, you announce it. A candidate trend article practically writes itself.

It keeps the blog active without requiring real strategy.

But AI doesn't recommend companies because they won an award. AI recommends companies that published real answers to buyer questions.

A blog full of press releases and candidate tips looks busy. It's just not functional.

Pull up your last 20 articles. How many were written for the person who signs the contract?

ACCA 4 exams. 1 month. 1 job. 1 diagnosed mental illness. 0 tutors.

Still reading? Good. 👏 

Because this isn't a success story. 😃 
It's a survival story — with receipts. 👍 

---

December 2025. 6 am. Alarm fires.

No tutor waiting. No class to attend. Just AI, my notes, and whatever focus I can scrape together before the day dismantles me.

8am — exam hall. I write for hours.
12pm — behind a retail counter. On my feet.
10pm — home. Hands tired. Brain still rehearsing the difference between a forward rate agreement and an interest rate swap.

I did that. Four times. In the same month.

---

What no one in that exam hall knew:

I was managing diagnosed clinical depression.
Living in a country that wasn't mine.
Sending money home. Losing final-round jobs because a visa costs more than a local hire — and nobody says that out loud.

Some days I sat down to study and felt completely hollow.

I opened the textbook anyway.

---

January 16, 2026. Results:

SBR — Strategic Business Reporting → 79 ✓
SBL — Strategic Business Leader → 64 ✓
APM — Advanced Performance Management → 56 ✓
AFM — Advanced Financial Management → 47 ✗

Three passes. One miss — by 3 marks.

I didn't celebrate. I stared at the 47 for days.

Maybe you know that feeling.

---

Then  SAVIN S — Senior Auditor at EY — said this:

"You cleared 3 strategic papers while managing depression, a physical job, zero support, and your own finances — abroad. That's not just passing exams. Post the story."

I'd been measuring myself against 50.
He made me count everything I was carrying.

---

What those marks actually mean in a finance role:

SBR 79 → I read financial statements the way a lawyer reads a contract. IFRS, consolidations, financial instruments — I don't flinch.

SBL 64 → Every number lives inside a strategy. I'm trained to see both.

APM 56 → KPIs, performance frameworks, divisional analysis — the daily language of FP&A. Passed while working retail shifts.

AFM 47 → Valuations, hedging, M&A, treasury. I know this material. The 47 was circumstance, not capability. It gets retaken.

---

One thing I'll share openly:

I used AI throughout prep — not as a shortcut, but as a method. I built practice financial datasets, stress-tested valuations, interpreted ratios, and challenged my own reasoning until it held.

That's how I work with financial data now.

If you want to know exactly how — DM me or drop a comment. Happy to share the full method.

---

I'm looking for roles in Finance · FP&A · Audit · Financial Analysis.

Senior leaders and hiring managers — I'd genuinely value a conversation.

And if you're in your own December right now — carrying things nobody can see, showing up anyway —

It's worth it.

Your results are coming.

💬 What's one thing you survived that you never gave yourself credit for?

Drop it below ⬇️

Committed architecture versus adaptive architecture.

A large GCC bank with a heavy AI CoE may look ahead today. But if its stack choices are too tightly coupled to one model provider, one cloud, one orchestration framework, one internal platform, or one consulting-led methodology, it may become less competitive in 24–36 months.

Meanwhile, a smaller bank that focuses on clean data, API readiness, workflow redesign, consent, modular product layers, and vendor-neutral orchestration may outperform without having made the early expensive bets.

The strategic principle should be:

In an unstable AI market, the winning bank is not the one with the biggest AI Centre of Excellence. It is the one with the lowest cost of changing its AI architecture.

That is the board-level insight.

The biggest AI risk for banks may not be underinvestment.

It may be premature commitment.

Many large banks have spent the last two years building AI Centres of Excellence, selecting platforms, hiring teams, creating internal frameworks, and standardising early AI stacks.

On paper, that looks progressive.

But the AI stack is still under heavy flux.

Models are changing. Agent frameworks are changing. Orchestration layers are changing. Inference economics are changing. Governance expectations are changing. Even the definition of “enterprise AI” is still moving.

That creates a risk many boards are not yet discussing clearly:

AI sunk-cost lock-in.

A bank may invest heavily in today’s AI architecture only to discover that its stack, tools, controls, or operating model are no longer competitive in two years.

And once the bank has built teams, processes, governance, vendor contracts, and internal politics around that stack, changing direction becomes difficult.

This is where smaller banks and neo banks may have an unexpected advantage.

They may not have the same AI budgets.

But they also do not carry the same AI legacy.

They can stay modular, wait for the stack to mature, adopt better tools faster, and avoid being trapped by yesterday’s architecture decisions.

The real question for banks is not:

“How much are we investing in AI?”

It is:

“How easily can we change our AI architecture when the market shifts?”

In this phase, optionality may matter more than scale.

The winning bank may not be the one with the biggest AI Centre of Excellence.

It may be the one with the lowest cost..

Banks that rushed to build AI factories may discover they have built the next generation of legacy.

A simple post for this sunny Friday.

if you are out there searching, applying and interviewing for a new job.

Whether you have been laid off, are between roles, had a contract end or maybe just not happy where you are.

I see you.
I hear you.
I feel for you.

There is so much noise right now. People telling you what your doing wrong, telling you its a terrible market, AI is taking your job etc...

Then there's the people trying to sell you a solution, people ghosting you and worst of all people trying to scam you!

In short it is exhausting and mentally draining.

So I just want to acknowledge that.

If you have any role in a hiring or interviewing process; as a hiring manager, HR/Talent professional or a recruiter. Please remember that the person on the other side is going through all of that. Give them a little grace and patience. Think about how it would feel if you were in that seat.

If you are the one looking for your new role try and give yourself a little grace and be kind to yourself. Yes put the effort in, do the research and show up to the interviews and be your best self. But take some time to step away and give yourself some breathing room.

When it comes to getting a solid contract to protect your coaching or consulting business, you have 2 options:

Option 1 - DIY it - borrow from a friend, ask ChatGPT, buy a random template, cobble it together from your mentor's agreements, OR
Option 2 -  Pay a lawyer like me $3,000+ to write it for you

This may sound familiar: 

“I hired a lawyer to write my contract, but now my offer is different, AI is changing everything, and I’m not sure it even works any more. Total waste of money.”

I hear some version of this story all. the. time. 

Unless you’re making high six-figures in your business, hiring a lawyer to create a custom contract rarely makes financial sense. 

The cost cuts DEEP into your profits. And it WILL need to be updated in a year or less.

The legal landscape in 2026 looks nothing like it did two years ago. 

I’d much rather see you spend your money on a template that actually makes sense for YOUR business, but the problem is - how do you know you filled it out the RIGHT way?

And what will you do when a potential client asks a question, or a former customer comes back asking for a refund?

What most business owners need is something in between - MORE than just a template, but LESS than hiring a lawyer for a custom contract.

That's why I created the Fine Print Academy - especially for established coaches and consultants.  

Weekly live guidance from the legal team that Amy Porterfield and Corinne Crabtree keep on retainer, 30+ attorney-drafted contracts, and real answers when something comes up in your business.

Right now you can enroll in the Fine Print Academy™ and devote just a few hours (literally) to setting up your business’s legal foundation. 

There’s no way to “fall behind” - you get exactly WHAT you need from it WHEN you need, because you have access to our firm’s legal team’s for live, human support. 

We’re raising the price to $199/month on April 25 - but right now, you can get it for $150 off your first three months, just $149/month.

69% of CEOs, CFOs, and senior executives use AI less than an hour a week. 28% don't use it at all. This is from a Stanford economist who surveyed 6,000+ senior leaders across four countries. The same study found that half of those executives believe their job is at risk if they don't get AI right this year.

The instinct is to read that as a motivation problem. I don't think it is.
AI is hard to use well if you don't already know what good looks like in your domain. If you can't tell whether the output is right, you can't trust it. If you can't trust it, you stop using it. The gap isn't willpower. It's the knowledge base underneath.

That's the thing I keep coming back to after using it daily across finance, people ops, capital work, contracts and board communications.

Here's what I actually found:

On finance: The speed gain isn't in the analysis. It's in the assembly. The part that used to eat three days is no longer the bottleneck. But if you can't read a P&L and immediately sense when something looks structurally off, you'll never catch what the model gets wrong.

On hiring and people decisions: AI exposed how underspecified my own frameworks were. You can't prompt your way to a good hiring scorecard if you haven't decided what you're actually optimising for. Useful lesson. Uncomfortable to learn.

On capital work: Valuation complex modeling, term sheet review, investor research. The output quality tracks almost perfectly to the clarity of the question going in. I've watched the same prompt produce something solid in one context and completely miss in another. The variable wasn't the tool.

On contracts: AI doesn't get tired on page 12 with still 100 pages to go in three different files. The real advantage is consistency, not intelligence. It still doesn't know which clause the other side will push on. That part I still have to bring.

On board and stakeholder communication: The drafting is faster. What improved more was the thinking. Structuring an argument for an audience forces a clarity you don't always have before you sit down to write it.

The quality ceiling, every time, is own thinking. Not the tool. If you're sitting on the fence: the reps are the only way through. The knowledge base you've spent years building is exactly what makes the tool useful. That's not a reason to wait. It's the reason to start.

Where are you finding that your existing domain knowledge changes what AI can do for you?

The future of hiring in India isn’t heading toward a slowdown—it’s heading toward a structural reset. The big shift is not “more jobs vs fewer jobs,” but how, where, and why companies hire.
Here’s a grounded, no-hype view of what’s actually changing:
1) From mass hiring → precision hiring
India used to rely heavily on volume hiring (especially IT services). That model is fading.
Companies are now prioritizing high-impact roles over headcount growth - Foundit 
1)Hiring is becoming ROI-driven—every role must justify business value
👉 Translation:
Fewer generic jobs, more specialized roles.
2) Skills are replacing degrees (quietly but decisively)
AI, data, product thinking, and cross-functional skills are rising
👉 Expect:
Portfolio-based hiring
Skill tests > CV screening
Non-traditional candidates getting more opportunities
3) AI is reshaping—not destroying—jobs
AI is changing hiring in two ways:
a) Fewer entry-level, repetitive roles
Automation is already affecting early-career jobs - The Times of India
b) Surge in high-skill roles
AI, ML, data, and product roles are booming
But experienced talent is still scarce (creating premium salaries)
4) India is becoming a global hiring hub
This is a huge macro trend.
India ranks among the top countries globally for hiring intent -India Briefing
Global companies are increasingly building teams here.
Even during downturns, hiring often shifts to India (cost + talent advantage)
👉 Why India wins:
Large talent pool
Cost efficiency
Strong digital infrastructure
5) Rise of Tier-2 and remote talent
Hiring is no longer metro-centric.
Companies are expanding into Tier-2 & Tier-3 cities - Business Standard
Remote and hybrid work has normalized distributed teams
👉 Outcome:
Talent access improves
Competition increases (you’re now competing globally, not locally)
6) Gig economy & platform-based hiring will expand
Not all jobs will be “full-time corporate roles.”
Gig and platform work is growing across sectors - India Briefing
Even traditionally informal sectors are becoming digitized - The Week
👉 Expect:
Freelance + contract roles becoming mainstream
Portfolio careers (multiple income streams)
7) Gen Z is forcing companies to rethink hiring
This is subtle but powerful.
Younger employees switch jobs faster, increasing churn - The Economic Times 
They expect:
Flexibility
Faster growth
Meaningful work
👉 Companies are responding with:
Better employee experience
Faster hiring cycles
Stronger employer branding
8) India may add 10–12 million jobs in 2026 - The Economic Times
But:
Growth is controlled, not explosive
Companies are avoiding over-hiring mistakes of the past
So what does the “future” actually look like?
If you compress everything into one line:
👉 India will hire more people—but fewer “average” profiles.
Final thought
India is moving from a labor advantage economy → talent advantage economy.
That’s a big upgrade—but it also means:
Opportunity is increasing
So is competition

The CFO who says yes to everything is not a strategic partner. They're a liability.

CFO.com published a piece this week called "Why 2026 will reward CFOs who say yes."

The argument is that the winning finance chiefs this year will embrace visibility, AI, broader leadership mandates, and a more expansive definition of their role. Say yes to evolution. Say yes to learning. Say yes to technology and growth.

I agree with about half of it.

The half I disagree with is the framing.

Because the CFO's structural value in any organisation is not their willingness to say yes. It's their willingness to say no. Or more precisely: it's their willingness to say "the numbers don't support this" in a room where everyone else has already decided they want to do it.

That's an uncomfortable thing to be. I've been that person. The one who tables the cash flow projection that shows the hiring plan is six months ahead of the revenue curve. The one who asks what the gross margin looks like when the sales team is celebrating a big new contract. The one who flags the board pack narrative that has quietly drifted away from what the actuals actually show.

None of those conversations make you popular in the moment.

But they are the conversations that make a CFO worth having in the room.

The risk with the "say yes" framing is that it conflates agility with agreeableness. A CFO should absolutely evolve, adopt new tools, build a public voice, and operate across a broader strategic remit. I do all of those things and I think they matter. But that evolution is only valuable if the underlying judgment remains independent.

The moment a CFO starts saying yes to everything, they've traded in the one thing that makes them different from everyone else at the table.

The CEO has commercial pressure pulling them toward optimism. The sales team has quota. The founder has vision and ego and four years of sunken cost in an idea. The CFO has the numbers, and the obligation to say what the numbers say, even when it's not what the room wants to hear.

2026 does reward CFOs who evolve. But the ones who will matter most are not the most agreeable ones.

They're the ones who've stayed honest.

#CFO #FinanceLeadership #Leadership

🚀 What Top Companies Are Really Hiring for in Senior Java Full Stack Developers (2026 Market Insights)
After spending 10+ years building enterprise applications, one thing is very clear in today’s market—
the bar for “Senior” has significantly evolved.
It’s no longer about knowing Java, Spring Boot, or a frontend framework.
Recruiters and hiring managers are now looking for engineers who can design, scale, and own complex systems end-to-end.
Here’s what’s truly driving hiring decisions right now 👇

🔹 1. Microservices + Event-Driven Architecture
Companies are prioritizing developers who can build scalable, loosely coupled systems using Spring Boot and tools like Kafka or RabbitMQ.
Real-time processing and system resilience are key.

🔹 2. Cloud-Native Development is Mandatory
Hands-on experience with AWS, Azure, or GCP, along with Docker & Kubernetes, is expected.
It’s not just deployment—it's about designing for scalability, fault tolerance, and cost efficiency.

🔹 3. AI-Ready Application Development
A major shift in 2026—organizations are integrating AI into their platforms.
Developers who can extend Java applications with AI/LLM capabilities are getting strong attention.

🔹 4. Full Stack Ownership (Backend + Frontend)
Senior developers are expected to go beyond APIs—
➡️ Build robust backend services
➡️ Integrate seamlessly with Angular/React
➡️ Deliver complete user-focused solutions

🔹 5. DevOps + CI/CD Mindset
The expectation today is simple:
You build it, you deploy it, you support it.
Experience with Jenkins, Terraform, Git pipelines, and monitoring tools is highly valued.

🔹 6. Business Impact & Problem Solving
What truly differentiates candidates now is the ability to:
✔️ Make architectural decisions
✔️ Optimize performance at scale
✔️ Solve real business problems—not just write code

💡 Key Takeaway:
The market isn’t just competitive—it’s selective.
The most in-demand developers are those who combine:
✅ Strong Java & Microservices foundation
✅ Cloud + Containerization expertise
✅ Event-driven and real-time system experience
✅ AI awareness and adaptability
✅ End-to-end ownership mindset

Yesterday I was grateful to be invited to and attend MMC Ventures's meet-up, focused on Deploying AI Agents.

We heard from brilliant speakers: Sevi U. (MMC Ventures) Bill Leaver (Synthesia), Romain Sestier (StackOne), Henry Comfort (Geordie AI), and Nick Carmont (Mistral AI).

My main takeaways from the night:

💡The demo-to-production gap is still huge: The impressive bit is easy. Getting an agent to work reliably in a messy real-world environment, with real users, real data, and real compliance requirements (the hard bit!), is a completely different problem.

💡 The autonomy/control trade-off is the actual product decision: The winning products won't be the most autonomous. They'll be the ones enterprises actually trust, which means visibility into what the agent is doing, what it can access, and how to pull the brakes.

💡 Forward deployment is massively underrated: The bottleneck isn't model quality, it's context. Extracting real workflow knowledge from the people doing the job (not the buyers signing the contract) is still manual, still hard, and still one of the most defensible things a startup can build.

💡 Horizontal is crowded: Vertical is the opportunity. Enterprises are defaulting to incumbents for general-purpose tooling. Startups win when they're specific, benchmark-driven, and genuinely better at one painful workflow than any platform product could be.

💡 Technology is improving faster than ROI is being captured: The next breakout AI companies will probably look less like model wrappers and more like workflow specialists; narrow focus, strong evals, enterprise-grade trust.

From a hiring perspective, two things jumped out:

👉🏻 The forward deployment point maps directly onto something I see in searches right now: founders struggling to articulate what "good" looks like for an early AI engineer hire, because the actual value isn't in the model, it's in the workflow context around it. The engineers who can extract that knowledge and turn it into something production-ready are the most sought after right now.

👉🏻 And the vertical vs horizontal point has a talent equivalent: Engineers moving into start-ups want to see defensibility from the likes of OpenAI and Claude releases.

I spoke with some awesome investors, founders and operators - a room full of brilliant individuals. Congrats to Georgia Higgs and Advika Jalan for hosting a great event 👏

Imagine walking into a bank and demanding a ₹1,358 crore loan.

No collateral. 0% interest. And you tell them you’ll pay it back in "services" whenever you get around to it.

The bank would laugh you out of the building. But for Info Edge (Naukri.com)? That’s just a standard Tuesday. 💼

I was deep-diving into their latest Annual Report and found a "secret" metric most people ignore because they’re too busy looking at the P&L: Deferred Sales Revenue.

That figure now stands at a massive ₹1,358 crore—up 19.3% YoY. 📈

Why is this the ultimate financial flex?

In simple terms, this is cash paid upfront by customers for services not yet rendered. It’s a "float" that acts as a lethal strategic weapon:

Interest-Free Fuel: While other tech startups burn equity to buy runway, Info Edge is effectively borrowing from its own customers. For free.

The Ultimate Shield: The global hiring market is "modest" right now. 

Recruitment cycles are slowing. But this pre-booked mountain of cash insulates them from the chaos.

Funding the Future: This safety net allows management to ignore quarterly noise. They are betting BIG on a dedicated AI team and the 'Naukri Talent Cloud' because the bill is already paid.

The Real Takeaway:
Customers don't lock in multi-year contracts and tie up significant capital unless there is deep trust. Info Edge hasn't just built a platform; they’ve built a financial fortress.

Most companies have a product. Info Edge has a moat made of ₹1,358 crore in "pre-paid" loyalty. 🏰

Did you know the "Balance Sheet" held this much power?

Most in-house legal teams have bolted AI onto a 2015 org chart and called it transformation.

I say this as someone who works in an in-house team and also builds software. I'm not throwing stones.
  
But here's what I'm seeing: the vast majority of in-house legal functions have adopted AI in the same way: a Copilot-style tool that sits alongside the existing team, speeds up drafting, helps with research, maybe summarises long contracts.
  
That's augmentation. It's useful. It's not transformation.
  
The structure underneath is unchanged. The same hierarchy. The same workflow. Legal as a function that reacts to the business rather than anticipates it. The same bottlenecks, just slightly faster.
  
The clearance agent conversation I wrote about applies directly here.
  
When you genuinely reimagine an in-house legal department, the shape of it changes completely. Routine contract review largely disappears as a human task. First-draft advice, same. What's left, and what grows, is judgment, strategy, and governance.
  
But here's the issue: most legal teams are planning for the augmented version, not the reimagined one. And those require very different hiring decisions, very different capability investment, very different structures.
  
The organisations that figure this out first won't just have more efficient legal teams. They'll have a genuine competitive advantage in how they manage risk.
  
I'll write about what those new roles actually look like next week.

You want an HR AI Expert that combines policy + process + system + compliance. That’s what gets adoption and budget.

⸻

🧠 1. WHAT YOUR KNOWLEDGE BASE SHOULD ACTUALLY INCLUDE

⚖️ A. UK Employment Law (baseline, not the product)

Include:
	•	Employment Rights Act 1996
	•	Equality Act 2010
	•	Working Time Regulations 1998
	•	National Minimum Wage Act 1998
	•	Data Protection Act 2018

👉 But don’t stop here. This alone won’t sell.

⸻

🏢 B. COMPANY HR POLICIES (this is where value is)

This is your gold:
	•	Leave policies (annual, sick, parental)
	•	Disciplinary procedures
	•	Grievance processes
	•	Remote working rules
	•	Expense policies
	•	Onboarding / offboarding checklists

👉 Why this matters:
Employees don’t ask “what does the law say?”
They ask:

“Can I carry over leave?”
“What happens if I’m off sick?”

⸻

⚙️ C. HR SYSTEM KNOWLEDGE (your edge)

Tie it to:
	•	PeopleSoft
	•	SAP SuccessFactors

Include:
	•	How to hire
	•	How to update job data
	•	How to request leave
	•	How to fix issues
      •     Include Precedents 

👉 This is what removes support tickets.

⸻

📊 D. HR PROCESSES (make it actionable)
	•	Hiring workflows
	•	Promotions
	•	Performance reviews
	•	Terminations
	•	Payroll cycles

👉 Turn these into step-by-step playbooks.

⸻

🧾 E. DOCUMENTS & TEMPLATES
	•	Contracts
	•	Offer letters
	•	Disciplinary letters
	•	Warning templates
	•	Exit forms

👉 Your AI should generate these instantly.

⸻

🚨 F. RISK & COMPLIANCE (this is your premium upsell)
	•	GDPR handling of employee data
	•	AI usage policies
	•	Audit trails
	•	Legal disclaimers 

ACCESS MODEL (THIS IS WHERE YOU NEED TO DECIDE)

You asked a very important question here. Most people mess this up.

⸻

OPTION 1. Give ALL employees access

✅ Pros:
	•	Reduces HR tickets massively
	•	High adoption
	•	Easy to sell

❌ Cons:
	•	Risk of misuse
	•	Needs guardrails
	•	Must be very simple

👉 Use for:
	•	Policy questions
	•	Leave queries
	•	Basic HR processes

⸻

OPTION 2. Intranet + AI Bot (BEST MODEL)

👉 This is the one you want.

Structure:
	•	HR portal (SharePoint / website)
	•	Knowledge base pages
	•	AI Expert in bottom corner

Users:
	•	Browse → read
	•	Ask → clarify

👉 Why this wins:
	•	Controlled environment
	•	Context-aware answers
	•	Scalable

⸻

OPTION 3. HR-only / power users

👉 Use this for:
	•	Sensitive processes
	•	Legal scenarios
	•	Data-heavy tasks

⸻

🔥 RECOMMENDED SETUP (DO THIS)

Don’t overthink it. Do this:

Layer 1. Employee AI
	•	Leave
	•	Policies
	•	Basic HR questions

Layer 2. Manager AI
	•	Hiring
	•	Performance
	•	Disciplinary guidance

Layer 3. HR AI (premium)
	•	Legal risk
	•	Complex cases
	•	Data + system fixes

⸻

💡 WHAT YOU’RE REALLY BUILDING

Not:
“An HR chatbot”

But:

👉 “A 24/7 HR Operations Engine that replaces repetitive HR work”

This can be set up in about 30 minutes. DM me or book a call to discuss options.

Mandatory skills- Ms Dynamics 365 Development, Customer Service, Copilot or AI,  Azure, Power Platform, Any MS Dynamics or Power Platform Certification
Look for MS dynamics 365 developer not for consultant



Job Description:

We are seeking a Mid- level Dynamics 365 Developer with foundational experience in Dynamics 365 Customer Service and interest in Microsoft Copilot / AI capabilities. The role focuses on supporting development activities, learning best practices, and contributing to solution delivery under the guidance of senior team members in an Agile environment.
Key Responsibilities

Support the development and customization of Dynamics 365 Customer Service solutions
Implement configurations such as entities, forms, views, workflows, and business rules
Assist with Copilot (AI-powered) feature configuration and enhancements
Build and support solutions using Power Platform (Power Apps, Power Automate, PowerPages/PowerPortal)
Support integrations and data flows with guidance from senior developers
Participate in Agile ceremonies including sprint planning, reviews, and retrospectives
Assist with testing, documentation, and defect resolution
Learn and follow coding standards, security models, and best practices
Required Qualifications

Bachelor’s degree in Computer Science, IT, or a related field
Microsoft Dynamics 365 or Power Platform certification
5+ years of experience with Dynamics 365 CE / Customer Service (or strong hands-on exposure)
Basic understanding of Dataverse, security roles, and case management concepts
Exposure to Power Platform (Power Apps, Power Automate)
Willingness to learn AI-driven features such as Dynamics 365 Copilot

Nice to Have

Exposure to Azure services or APIs
Experience working in Agile teams
Good communication and collaboration skills

• Location: Remote (India)
• Experience: 7+ Years
• Duration: 6 Months (Extendable)

• Develop and manage end-to-end ML pipelines (preprocessing → training → deployment → monitoring)

• Collaborate with cross-functional teams for seamless model integration
• Drive continuous improvements in ML lifecycle & MLOps practices

• Proven experience deploying Python-based ML models in production

⭐ Nice to Have:
• Exposure to Digital Oilfield / OT-IT integration
• Experience with Azure cloud services
• Familiarity with real-time streaming (Kafka, IoT platforms)
• Understanding of enterprise security & compliance frameworks

🎓 Certifications (Plus):
• Azure DevOps / Azure AI Engineer
• AWS DevOps Engineer

Interested candidates can send their resume to gurudatta.kavale@softbay.com

Role- MS Dynamics 365 Developer

Type of Hire- TPC

interested candidates share CV 
bushra.parveen@follextechnology.com

Mandatory skills- Ms Dynamics 365 Development, Customer Service, Copilot or AI,  Azure, Power Platform, Any MS Dynamics or Power Platform Certification
Look for MS dynamics 365 developer not for consultant



Job Description:

We are seeking a Mid- level Dynamics 365 Developer with foundational experience in Dynamics 365 Customer Service and interest in Microsoft Copilot / AI capabilities. The role focuses on supporting development activities, learning best practices, and contributing to solution delivery under the guidance of senior team members in an Agile environment.
Key Responsibilities

Support the development and customization of Dynamics 365 Customer Service solutions
Implement configurations such as entities, forms, views, workflows, and business rules
Assist with Copilot (AI-powered) feature configuration and enhancements
Build and support solutions using Power Platform (Power Apps, Power Automate, PowerPages/PowerPortal)
Support integrations and data flows with guidance from senior developers
Participate in Agile ceremonies including sprint planning, reviews, and retrospectives
Assist with testing, documentation, and defect resolution
Learn and follow coding standards, security models, and best practices
Required Qualifications

Bachelor’s degree in Computer Science, IT, or a related field
Microsoft Dynamics 365 or Power Platform certification
5+ years of experience with Dynamics 365 CE / Customer Service (or strong hands-on exposure)
Basic understanding of Dataverse, security roles, and case management concepts
Exposure to Power Platform (Power Apps, Power Automate)
Willingness to learn AI-driven features such as Dynamics 365 Copilot

Nice to Have

Exposure to Azure services or APIs
Experience working in Agile teams
Good communication and collaboration skills
